questions.q: An assistant must answer from a set of documents the base LLM was never trained on. What does RAG do?
options: Retrieve the matching document passages, then generate from them
Generate the answer from the model's own memory without lookup
Retrain the whole model on the documents before each question
Match the question to a keyword index and return raw text
questions.explain: RAG retrieves relevant text, then the LLM generates an answer grounded in that text.

questions.q: When are answers best handled by RAG rather than a plain LLM?
options: The documents are short enough for the model to memorize fully
The answers sit in private documents the base model never saw
RAG mainly makes the model respond faster than a plain LLM
RAG lets you skip using a language model entirely here
questions.explain: RAG fits when the knowledge is in your own documents that aren't in the model's training data.

questions.q: Put the RAG build steps in the order the notebook uses them.
options: Generate, retrieve, embed, chunk, then load the documents
Embed, generate, load, chunk, then store the vectors
Load, chunk, embed, store, then retrieve and generate
Chunk, generate, load, retrieve, then embed the text
questions.explain: The order is: load docs, chunk, embed, store in the vector DB, then retrieve and generate.

questions.q: Long documents are split into 'chunks' before indexing. Why?
options: Smaller text is simply easier for a person to read
The vector database rejects files larger than one kilobyte
Chunking automatically translates the text into English
Small pieces retrieve precisely and fit the context window
questions.explain: Chunking returns only relevant passages and keeps pieces within the model's context window.

questions.q: If CHUNK_SIZE is set very large (a whole document per chunk), what is the main downside?
options: Chunks carry irrelevant text, adding noise and token cost
The embeddings suddenly become perfectly accurate
The vector database refuses to store the large chunks
The final answer is then guaranteed to be correct
questions.explain: Chunks that are too large bring unrelated text, adding noise and cost.

questions.q: What is the purpose of CHUNK_OVERLAP between consecutive chunks?
options: It shrinks each chunk further to save on memory usage
It keeps context that sits on a chunk boundary from being cut
It shuffles the order of the text inside a chunk at random
It finds and deletes duplicate documents from the set
questions.explain: Overlap repeats a little text at boundaries so an idea split across chunks is still findable.

questions.q: Each chunk is turned into an embedding. What is an embedding?
options: A compressed ZIP archive of the whole document
The English translation of the original text chunk
A vector of numbers that represents the text's meaning
A random identifier that is assigned to each chunk
questions.explain: An embedding is a numeric vector capturing meaning; similar meaning gives similar vectors.

questions.q: Why embed the text instead of matching keywords?
options: It only works for images and never for plain text
Keyword matching cannot be done in Python at all
It makes each stored document noticeably shorter
It matches by meaning even if the words differ
questions.explain: Embeddings enable semantic search — matching by meaning, not exact keywords.

questions.q: The lab sets EMBEDDING_MODEL = "text-embedding-3-small". What is its job?
options: Turn text into meaning-vectors for similarity search
Write the final natural-language answer for the user
Store the finished vectors safely onto the disk
Split the long documents into smaller text chunks
questions.explain: text-embedding-3-small converts text into vectors; generation is a separate model.

questions.q: When a question comes in, why embed it with the SAME model used for the chunks?
options: It is only a matter of personal coding style choice
Similarity only holds if both share the same vector space
Different models are faster but give identical vectors
The question does not need to be embedded at all
questions.explain: Query and documents must use the same embedding model so their vectors are comparable.

questions.q: The lab uses ChromaDB with get_or_create_collection(...). What does this vector database do?
options: Run the language model that writes the final answers
Render the Gradio interface that is shown to the user
Store chunk embeddings and find ones similar to a query
Download the source documents directly from the internet
questions.explain: ChromaDB stores embeddings and performs nearest-neighbor similarity search.

questions.q: Why store chunks in a vector database instead of a normal SQL text table?
options: A normal SQL table cannot store any text at all
Vector databases are always cheaper to run than SQL
SQL databases delete their stored data on their own
It is built for similarity search over embeddings
questions.explain: Vector DBs are optimized for nearest-neighbor search over embeddings.

questions.q: collection.add(embeddings=..., documents=..., ids=...) runs after embedding. What just happened?
options: Each chunk, its embedding, and an id become searchable
The final answer is delivered to the waiting user
The source documents are removed from the collection
The model is fine-tuned on the newly added chunks
questions.explain: collection.add indexes chunks with embeddings and ids so they can be retrieved.

questions.q: The code embeds the question, then calls collection.query(query_embeddings=[...]). What is this?
options: Write the full answer using the GPT generation model
Embed the question, then find the closest stored chunks
Retrain the embedding model on the user's question
Delete the whole collection before answering anything
questions.explain: Retrieval embeds the question, then queries the vector DB for the closest chunks.

questions.q: The lab sets TOP_K = 8 and passes n_results=top_k. What does TOP_K control?
options: How many words the final answer is allowed to contain
How many times the model gets fine-tuned per query
How many similar chunks are retrieved as context
The temperature setting used by the generation model
questions.explain: TOP_K / n_results is the number of nearest chunks passed to the generator.

questions.q: A student sets TOP_K = 1. What risk does this create?
options: It guarantees the fastest and fullest possible answers
It makes the resulting embeddings far more accurate
It forces the model to hallucinate the answer on purpose
One chunk may miss context that the answer needs
questions.explain: Too small a TOP_K can miss relevant context; too large adds noise. It's a balance.

questions.q: After retrieval, chunks go into the prompt and gpt-4o-mini is called. What is the generation step?
options: The LLM writes an answer from the retrieved chunks
The LLM stores new embeddings back into ChromaDB
The LLM splits the documents into smaller chunks
The LLM computes the cosine similarity scores
questions.explain: Generation is where the LLM composes the final answer from the retrieved context.

questions.q: Why does the prompt tell the model to answer using the retrieved context, not its memory?
options: To make the final answer noticeably longer for the user
To ground the answer in documents and cut hallucination
Because the generation model has no training data at all
To avoid having to use any embeddings in the pipeline
questions.explain: Grounding the answer in retrieved text keeps it faithful and reduces made-up facts.

questions.q: The documents are updated with new information. With RAG, what is the easiest correct fix?
options: Fine-tune the whole model again from scratch nightly
Rewrite the entire application in another language
Re-chunk and re-embed the new documents into the DB
Do nothing, since the model already knows the update
questions.explain: With RAG you just index new documents; no model retraining is needed.

questions.q: When is fine-tuning a better choice than RAG?
options: Whenever the source documents change very frequently
When you want to avoid any training cost whatsoever
When answers must be grounded in specific documents
When you need to change the model's style or behavior
questions.explain: Fine-tuning changes behavior baked into the weights; RAG supplies fresh grounded facts.

questions.q: This is the 'AI Prompt Edition' and students used an LLM to write code. Does that make it RAG?
options: No — coding with an LLM is separate from being RAG
Yes — any use of an LLM automatically counts as RAG
No — it is really a fine-tuning project underneath
Yes — writing code with AI is the meaning of retrieval
questions.explain: Using an LLM to help write code is unrelated; it's RAG because it retrieves then generates.

questions.q: A user's question returns an irrelevant answer. Which cause fits the RAG pipeline best?
options: The temporary Gradio share link simply expired
Retrieval pulled the wrong chunks for the question
The embedding model translated the text by mistake
The vector database always returns random chunks
questions.explain: Bad RAG answers usually trace to retrieval — chunking, embeddings, or TOP_K bringing wrong context.
